In response to these limitations, the advent of IoT-based air quality monitoring systems has emerged as a promising solution by providing real-time data with higher spatio-temporal resolution, although they come with their own challenges, such as lower accuracy and data quality concerns (Buelvas et al., 2023). Real-time IoT-powered AI system for monitoring and forecasting of air pollution in industrial environment
=========================================================================================================

Highlights
----------

*   •Novel IoT-based system for real-time air quality monitoring in chrome plating industry. 
*   •IoT sensors monitor pollutants like NH3, CO, NO2, CH4, CO2, SO2, O3, PM2.5, PM10. 
*   •AI models (LSTM, Random Forest, Linear Regression) predict pollution levels accurately. 
*   •System activates exhaust fans based on predicted pollution, preventing hazards. 
*   •LSTM model achieved 99 % R² and 0.33 MAE for temperature and humidity forecasting. 

Abstract
--------

Air pollution in industrial environments, particularly in the chrome plating process, poses significant health risks to workers due to high concentrations of hazardous pollutants. Exposure to substances like hexavalent chromium, volatile organic compounds (VOCs), and particulate matter can lead to severe health issues, including respiratory problems and lung cancer. Continuous monitoring and timely intervention are crucial to mitigate these risks. Traditional air quality monitoring methods often lack real-time data analysis and predictive capabilities, limiting their effectiveness in addressing pollution hazards proactively. This paper introduces a real-time air pollution monitoring and forecasting system specifically designed for the chrome plating industry. The system, supported by Internet of Things (IoT) sensors and AI approaches, detects a wide range of air pollutants, including NH 3, CO, NO 2, CH 4, CO 2, SO 2, O 3, PM2.5, and PM10, and provides real-time data on pollutant concentration levels. Data collected by the sensors are processed using LSTM, Random Forest, and Linear Regression models to predict pollution levels. The LSTM model achieved a coefficient of variation (R²) of 99 % and a mean absolute percentage error (MAE) of 0.33 for temperature and humidity forecasting. For PM2.5, the Random Forest model outperformed others, achieving an R² of 84 % and an MAE of 10.11. The system activates factory exhaust fans to circulate air when high pollution levels are predicted to occur in the next hours, allowing for proactive measures to improve air quality before issues arise. This innovative approach demonstrates significant advancements in industrial environmental monitoring, enabling dynamic responses to pollution and improving air quality in industrial settings.

1. Introduction
---------------

### 1.1. Background

Air pollution is a pressing environmental issue with profound implications for human health and ecosystems, exacerbated significantly by industrial activities. Industrial emissions release a variety of hazardous substances, including particulate matter (PM2.5 and PM10), sulfur oxides (SOx), nitrogen oxides (NOx), carbon monoxide (CO), VOCs, and heavy metals, which degrade air quality and pose severe health risks ([Kumar and Rajput, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib25)). These pollutants contribute to respiratory and cardiovascular diseases, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), and even cancer, particularly affecting workers in industrial environments who are exposed to higher concentrations of these toxic substances ([Singh and Singh, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib46), [Hormati et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib17)). For instance, in the Upper Silesian Region of Poland, exposure to PM2.5 and PM10 was found to be several times higher than the national average, correlating with increased premature deaths and years of life lost due to air pollution ([Połednik, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib39)). Similarly, in Romania, significant concentrations of arsenic and PM2.5 were recorded, posing major health risks, especially to vulnerable populations like children ([Tanasa et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib47)). The adverse effects of air pollution extend beyond human health, impacting plant productivity, ecosystem biodiversity, and contributing to global phenomena such as climate change, ozone depletion, and acid rain ([Agrawal and Agrawal, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib1)). In mining areas, emissions from heavy motor vehicles and mining activities further exacerbate air pollution, leading to severe health issues such as lung cancer and respiratory disorders among workers and nearby residents ([Kumar and Rajput, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib25)). Effective regulations, policies, and technological interventions are crucial to mitigate these risks and protect both human health and the environment from the detrimental effects of industrial air pollution ([Singh et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib45)). Therefore, addressing industrial air pollution is imperative for ensuring sustainable development and safeguarding public health and ecosystems.

Despite the known dangers of air pollution in industrial settings, traditional air quality monitoring methods are often inadequate. These methods typically lack real-time data analysis and predictive capabilities, making it difficult to implement timely interventions. Consequently, there is a pressing need for more advanced monitoring solutions that can provide immediate feedback and predict future pollution levels to prevent hazardous conditions. This study aims to develop a comprehensive real-time air pollution monitoring and forecasting system tailored for the chrome plating industry. The primary objective is to mitigate health risks associated with high pollutant concentrations by providing accurate, real-time data and predictive insights into air quality. To address the limitations of traditional monitoring methods, we propose a system that integrates IoT sensors with advanced AI models. This system is designed to detect and measure various air pollutants, including NH 3, CO, NO 2, CH 4, CO 2, SO 2, O 3, PM2.5, and PM10, and to provide real-time data on their concentration levels. Our approach stands out by combining IoT and AI technologies to offer both real-time monitoring and predictive analytics. The system utilizes LSTM networks, Random Forest algorithms, and Linear Regression models to forecast pollution levels accurately. This predictive capability allows for proactive measures, such as activating factory exhaust fans before pollution levels become hazardous, thus ensuring a safer working environment.

### 1.2. Literature review

Air quality monitoring has evolved significantly over the years, driven by the increasing awareness of the detrimental effects of air pollution on human health and the environment. Historically, the need for air quality monitoring became evident after severe contamination events, which spurred the development of various technologies and methodologies to measure atmospheric pollutants ([Vélez-Guerrero et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib50)). Initially, monitoring efforts were rudimentary, relying on basic observational techniques and simple instruments. However, as industrialization, urbanization, and motorized transport surged, particularly in developed countries like the USA, Japan, and Germany, the complexity and sophistication of monitoring systems increased. These countries adopted automatic continuous monitoring instruments based on specific physicochemical or electrochemical properties of pollutants, although these systems were often too costly and complex for widespread use in developing nations ([Gupta and Singh, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib14)). The advent of the IoT has revolutionized air quality monitoring, enabling real-time data collection and analysis through interconnected sensors and devices. Modern systems now utilize a variety of sensors, such as MQ135, MQ6, MQ7, and MQ2, to detect harmful gases like NO 2, CO, NH 3, and benzene, and relay this data to microcontrollers and online servers for continuous monitoring and alerting ([Alekhya et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib2)). Additionally, innovative methodologies, such as the geospatial data analysis used in Cluj-Napoca, have improved the spatial and temporal resolution of monitoring networks, making them more efficient and cost-effective ([Camarasan et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib6)). In developing countries, simpler and more affordable monitoring systems based on USEPA concepts have been adapted to local conditions, ensuring broader accessibility and usability ([Gupta and Singh, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib14)). The integration of historical data and advanced modeling techniques has further enhanced the ability to estimate long-term pollutant concentrations, crucial for epidemiological studies on chronic diseases ([Michalik et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib35)).

Traditional air quality monitoring methods typically involve the use of robust, stationary monitoring stations that measure various pollutants such as CO 2, NO 2, NH 3, and benzene. These stations are highly accurate and provide reliable data, but they come with significant limitations. One major drawback is the high installation and maintenance costs, which restrict their deployment to larger cities and well-funded regions, leaving smaller cities and underdeveloped areas less covered ([Martinez et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib32)). Additionally, these traditional systems often require laboratory analysis, which results in poor precision and sensitivity in real-time monitoring ([Ganesh et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib10)). This lack of real-time data is a critical limitation, as it prevents timely responses to sudden changes in air quality, which can be detrimental to public health ([Kalaivani et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib22)). Furthermore, traditional methods lack predictive capabilities, making it difficult to forecast air quality trends and take preemptive measures. For instance, while HVAC systems can control indoor air quality, they often rely on extensive historical data to function effectively, which can be costly and may not adapt well to changing conditions ([Rescio et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib41)).

In response to these limitations, the advent of IoT-based air quality monitoring systems has emerged as a promising solution by providing real-time data with higher spatio-temporal resolution, although they come with their own challenges, such as lower accuracy and data quality concerns ([Buelvas et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib5)). These IoT systems use low-cost sensors and cloud-based platforms like ThingSpeak for data storage and visualization, enabling more accessible and widespread monitoring ([Karnati, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib24)). Despite their advantages, IoT systems still face issues related to data accuracy and the need for calibration to ensure reliable measurements ([Veerani et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib49)). Overall, while traditional air quality monitoring methods are reliable, their limitations in cost, real-time data provision, and predictive capabilities highlight the need for more advanced, cost-effective, and adaptive monitoring solutions.

Recent advancements in IoT technologies have addressed some of these challenges, significantly enhancing the effectiveness of air quality monitoring systems, providing real-time data and comprehensive analysis of various pollutants. IoT-based air quality monitoring systems utilize a range of sensors to detect harmful gases and compounds, such as CO 2, volatile organic compounds (VOCs), particulate matter, and other pollutants. For instance, the system designed by Hasbullah et al. employs sensors to measure temperature, humidity, CO 2, VOCs, and particulate matter, transmitting data to a microcontroller and notifying users via the Telegram application ([Hasbullah et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib15)). Similarly, Rahul A's study highlights the use of the Blynk app to fetch real-time pollution data from multiple locations, making it accessible on smartphones ([Naik et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib36)). Ziyodullayevna's research introduces a portable air quality detection device using MQ135 and MQ3 sensors, with data stored and visualized on the ThinkSpeak cloud-based web app, incorporating machine learning for data analysis ([Karnati, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib24)). The study by Kalaivani et al. discusses the integration of MQ2 and DHT11 sensors with the ESP32 microcontroller, utilizing the ThingSpeak platform for data logging and real-time alerts ([Kalaivani et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib22)). Ganesh et al. propose a three-phase IoT-based pollution monitoring system that tracks pollution levels using an internet server, providing real-time monitoring and long-term analysis ([Ganesh et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib10)). Veerani et al. present an advanced real-time air quality reporting system using Arduino IDE, gas sensors (MQ135), and GPS for live location tracking, with data integrated into a mobile app via IoT ([Alekhya et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib2)). These studies collectively demonstrate the effectiveness of IoT sensors in monitoring air quality, offering real-time data, remote accessibility, and comprehensive pollutant analysis, thereby contributing to public health and environmental protection.

AI and ML have become pivotal in air quality monitoring and prediction, leveraging various models to enhance accuracy and efficiency. LSTM networks and Gated Recurrent Units (GRU) are recurrent neural networks (RNNs) that have shown significant promise in predicting nitrogen dioxide (NO 2) concentrations, outperforming traditional methods despite challenges with data quality and generalization capabilities ([Cican et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib7)).

The integration of IoT devices with cloud-based AI models, such as the Bayesian-optimized hybrid time-series (BO-HyTS) model combining Seasonal Autoregressive Integrated Moving Average (SARIMA) and LSTM, has further improved the accuracy of Air Quality Index (AQI) forecasts by capturing both linear and nonlinear data characteristics ([Ansari and Alam, 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib3)). Model Output Statistics (MOS) approaches using machine learning algorithms like Random Forest (RF) and Gradient Boosting have been applied to downscale regional air quality forecasts, with the global approach often outperforming local models in predicting pollutants like particulate matter (PM10 and PM2.5), ozone (O 3), and NO 2 ([Bertrand et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib4)).

The deployment of low-cost sensors in hybrid networks, calibrated through data-driven ML methods, has also enhanced spatial resolution and accuracy in air quality monitoring, addressing the limitations of traditional, expensive monitoring stations ([Vajs et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib48)). AI models such as RF, Backpropagation Neural Network (BPNN), and Genetic Algorithm (GA) have been used to explore the correlation between air quality and meteorological factors, with fusion models like RF + BP + GA providing highly accurate AQI predictions ([Liu et al., 2023a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib28)).

In the context of health impacts, ML models including linear regression, RF, AdaBoost, and neural networks have been employed to study the relationship between air pollutant concentrations and pediatric respiratory diseases, with RF emerging as the best-performing model ([Ji et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib20)). A comprehensive review of ML models for air quality forecasting highlights the widespread use of deep learning models over the past decade, emphasizing their geographical distribution, predicted values, and evaluation metrics ([Méndez et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib33)).

Encoder-decoder architectures with attention mechanisms have been proposed for forecasting PM levels, demonstrating superior performance by focusing on the most relevant data elements and addressing challenges in missing data through adversarial networks for data augmentation ([Kalajdjieski et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib23)). The AIrSense framework exemplifies the use of AI for anomaly detection and data cleaning in low-cost sensor networks, ensuring reliable pollutant concentration measurements ([Rollo et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib42)).

Finally, the Genetic Algorithm-based Kernel Extreme Learning Machine (GA-KELM) has been enhanced to predict various pollutants and AQI, outperforming conventional models like convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and recurrent neural networks (RNNs) in terms of training speed and prediction accuracy ([Liu et al., 2023a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib29)). These diverse applications of AI and ML models underscore their critical role in advancing air quality monitoring and prediction, providing valuable insights for environmental management and public health.

IoT and AI-based air quality monitoring systems have seen various real-world implementations, significantly impacting pollution management and worker health. For instance, a distributed sensing system using commercial sensors was deployed to monitor toxic compounds in a melting furnace environment, effectively detecting dangerous conditions for workers and enhancing safety measures ([Parri et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib37)). Another notable implementation is the hybrid sensor network combining public monitoring stations with low-cost devices for air quality monitoring, which demonstrated improved calibration accuracy and cost-effectiveness, making it feasible for large-scale deployments ([Vajs et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib48)).

Additionally, an IoT-Cloud-based model using the BO-HyTS approach was proposed to forecast the Air Quality Index (AQI) in different meteorological conditions, providing accurate predictions that can inform policy decisions and environmental management strategies ([Ansari and Alam, 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib3)). In industrial settings, a complete IoT-based air quality monitoring infrastructure was developed, integrating real-time data collection and machine learning techniques to predict safety level breaches, thus protecting both workers and the environment ([García et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib11)).

Despite these successes, several challenges and limitations persist. Sensor accuracy remains a significant technical challenge, particularly for low-cost sensors that are prone to errors and degradation over time. Intelligent calibration methods, such as those using Bayesian frameworks, have been proposed to address these issues, but they still face difficulties in maintaining accuracy during extreme events and detecting calibrator drift ([Zaidan et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib52)).

Data integration is another technical hurdle, as the high-dimensional, spatio-temporal data from numerous IoT devices require sophisticated models to analyze and forecast pollution levels accurately. Deep learning models with attention mechanisms have shown promise in this area, but they necessitate extensive computational resources and robust data augmentation techniques to handle missing data ([Kalajdjieski et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib23)).

Practical challenges include the cost and scalability of deploying these systems. While low-cost sensors offer a more affordable solution, their maintenance and calibration can be logistically complex, especially when deployed in large numbers ([Vajs et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib48)). The need for a flexible and modular calibration scheme, as proposed in the MAQ-CaF methodology, is crucial to ensure reliable and scalable air quality monitoring ([Hashmy et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib16)). Additionally, the rapid development of AIoT/IIoT/IoT technologies brings about challenges related to safety, security, latency, and interoperability, which are essential for the effective implementation of these systems in various applications, including Industry 4.0 and autonomous vehicles ([Hou et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib18)). Ethical considerations, such as potential biases in AI algorithms and the safeguarding of patient privacy in health-related applications, also pose significant challenges that need to be addressed to ensure the responsible use of AI in air quality monitoring ([Satapathy et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib44)). Overall, while IoT and AI-based air quality monitoring systems have demonstrated substantial benefits in pollution management and worker health, overcoming the technical and practical challenges is essential for their broader adoption and effectiveness. # Technologies for stationary air quality monitoring

# **Technologies for stationary air quality monitoring**

Stationary air quality monitoring technologies form the backbone of efforts to maintain public health and adhere to environmental regulations. These technologies provide continuous data essential for understanding and managing air pollution. Accurate monitoring is crucial for identifying pollution sources and trends, enabling decisive action to mitigate adverse health impacts.

Stationary monitoring systems are integral to comprehensive air quality management. They deliver reliable, precise data necessary for informed decision-making. Utilizing advanced sensors and data analytics, these systems offer insights into pollutant levels and distribution.

**Air quality monitoring technologies can support:**

Embracing stationary air monitoring technologies is vital for a healthier environment. By providing continuous, real-time data, these systems empower communities and authorities to respond proactively to air quality challenges.

## **Historical development of air quality monitoring technologies**

Air quality monitoring technologies have evolved significantly to provide precise data for environmental challenges. Early monitoring relied on manual sampling and lab analysis, which took too much time and offered limited scope.

Technology advancements have led to more efficient and accurate monitoring. Sensors and IoT devices now enable real-time data collection and analysis. These technologies increase spatial resolution and accessibility of air quality data, allowing detailed assessments of pollution sources and trends.

**Ambient air quality monitoring technology advancements include:**

## **Types of stationary air quality monitoring systems**

Stationary air quality monitoring systems include reference-grade monitors, low-cost sensors, and hybrid systems.

Reference-grade monitors are the gold standard. They provide precise measurements and are often used for regulatory purposes. These monitors are reliable and offer high accuracy, making them crucial for compliance with environmental standards. They are typically used at fixed locations to continuously gather data on critical air pollutants.

Low-cost sensors have gained popularity due to their affordability and flexibility. These sensors are not as precise as reference-grade monitors but provide valuable data over a wider area. They are ideal for supplementary monitoring, offering insights into pollution patterns in urban environments. Their cost-effectiveness makes them accessible for community projects and research initiatives. For detailed recommendations on optimizing deployment in urban settings, refer to our discussion on [best practices when deploying low-cost air sensors in the urban environment](https://www.clarity.io/blog/best-practices-when-deploying-low-cost-air-sensors-in-the-urban-environment).

Hybrid systems combine the strengths of both traditional monitors and low-cost sensors. These systems enhance monitoring capabilities by integrating various technologies. They offer a comprehensive view of air quality, utilizing data analytics to improve accuracy and detail. Hybrid systems are adaptable and scalable, providing insights necessary for informed policy-making and public health protection. This integration aligns with emerging [air quality management 2.0 methodologies](https://www.clarity.io/blog/air-quality-management-2-0-the-role-of-air-quality-monitoring-companies-and-air-pollution-measurement-technology).

These systems play a pivotal role in environmental monitoring. Each type serves specific needs, contributing to a holistic understanding of air quality and supporting efforts to reduce air pollution.

## **Integration of monitoring technologies with data management**

Stationary air quality monitoring technologies seamlessly integrate with sophisticated data management systems. Our advanced software plays a critical role in analyzing and visualizing air quality data allowing for real-time insights.

Modern data management systems are designed to handle vast amounts of environmental data efficiently. These systems organize and process data from various sensors, providing a comprehensive view of air quality. The use of IoT and [cloud-based platforms](https://www.clarity.io/air-quality-monitoring-solution/cloud) ensures that data is accessible and actionable.

Software solutions process raw data into meaningful insights. They enable [detailed analysis of pollutant levels and trends](https://www.clarity.io/blog/air-quality-management-2-0-how-researchers-drive-actionable-air-quality-intelligence-with-open-data-and-analysis). Visualization tools present data in intuitive formats, making complex information easier to understand for stakeholders.

Our integration of monitoring technologies with data management systems empowers communities and authorities to address air quality challenges effectively. This synergy ensures that data-driven strategies are both proactive and responsive.

## **Role of stationary monitoring in regulatory compliance**

Regulatory bodies rely on [stationary monitors for regulatory engagement](https://www.clarity.io/blog/air-quality-management-2-0-why-regulatory-engagement-is-key-to-driving-policy-outcomes-and-air-pollution-emissions-reductions) to gather precise data on air pollutants. This information establishes benchmarks and helps enforce regulations. By offering a consistent stream of data, these systems help identify areas where air quality standards are not met, prompting timely interventions.

Monitoring data supports policy-making by offering evidence-based insights. Policymakers use this data to create effective regulations and strategies. This ensures that air quality management aligns with scientific findings and public health goals. Detailed evaluations on [enhanced monitoring solutions shaping environmental policies](https://www.clarity.io/blog/maximizing-the-impact-of-environmental-policies-through-enhanced-air-quality-monitoring) provide additional context on how real-time data can drive meaningful outcomes.

## **Advancements in measurement and modeling technologies**

Recent advancements in pollutant measurement and computational modeling have significantly enhanced the capabilities of stationary air quality monitoring. These innovations ensure more precise, real-time data collection, enabling better analysis and understanding of air pollutants.

Emerging measurement technologies have improved the accuracy of pollutant detection. Advanced sensor capabilities now offer higher sensitivity and specificity, allowing for detailed identification of various pollutants under diverse environmental conditions. For example, our exploration of [low-cost air sensors for better air quality](https://www.clarity.io/blog/how-low-cost-air-sensors-empower-economically-developing-nations-to-improve-their-air-quality-monitoring-infrastructure) discusses how enhanced monitor sensitivity contributes to accurate detection at increasingly lower concentrations.

Computational modeling has evolved alongside measurement technologies. Advanced algorithms and machine learning techniques now process large datasets efficiently, offering deeper insights into air quality patterns. These models simulate pollutant dispersion and predict future air quality scenarios, supporting proactive policy-making and intervention strategies.Â

Emerging trends in stationary air quality monitoring are changing environmental sensing technology. Artificial Intelligence and real-time analytics now lead the way, making data processing and interpretation faster. .

AI algorithms improve data accuracy by learning from past patterns and current inputs. This lets us identify pollution sources and trends more precisely. Real-time analytics use these capabilities to give immediate feedback, providing authorities with actionable intelligence for quick interventions.

[Hybrid monitoring networks](https://www.clarity.io/blog/building-resilient-communities-through-effective-air-pollution-measurement) are also gaining popularity. Better sensors, IoT devices, and data analytics have upgraded air quality monitoring. Real-time data collection improves pollutant detection and enables quick responses. This helps maintain environmental compliance and protect public health.

Until I wrote about [my quest for good air](https://www.wired.com/story/the-quest-for-clean-air/) in my Brooklyn apartment, I didn’t know air quality monitors even existed. Now I couldn’t live without them. To become aware of your indoor air quality index is to enter a realm where the invisible becomes something you can never unsee.

We humans evolved to respond to changes in temperature, precipitation, and wind. Bad air is silent and often odorless, but long-term exposure to certain vapors, particulates, and high levels of CO2 can impact cognition and make us vulnerable to certain cancers, as well as heart and respiratory diseases. Reading the daily temperature and looking at weather forecasts prepares us for what’s to come, but checking your air quality might be the biggest step you make in improving your health.

These air quality monitors were tested in two locations: a 130-plus-year-old Brooklyn apartment with a gas stove in a building that is undergoing construction, and a cabin in the Maine woods that uses an electric stove. There were two cats, a dog, and two people during the entire testing period. I had various air purifiers running at all times. Neither location had central air or HVAC with MERV filters, nor did they have over-stove exhaust fans that could remove fumes to the outside. In both locations, it was cooking on the stovetop that produced the worst air. These monitors were used both on days with excellent outdoor air and days when air quality was in the moderate zone, above 50 on the [US Air Quality Index](https://www.airnow.gov/aqi/aqi-basics/).

When that apocalyptic orange cloud set over San Francisco in 2020, the [crowdsourced air quality index map PurpleAir](https://www.wired.com/story/bay-area-just-turned-orange-all-eyes-on-purpleair/) was the place to see how bad the air really was. [PurpleAir’s Real-Time Air Quality Map](https://www.wired.com/story/bay-area-just-turned-orange-all-eyes-on-purpleair/) is created by the public using sensors that PurpleAir sells. It offers two types of outdoor air monitors: the [PurpleAir Classic Air Quality Monitor](https://cna.st/affiliate-link/meh9fHuiYWLipnB6KA3ZTAM4tL2kCV2nNJBmBKdgep1ggjUdbaNyYEW9gy3bT8mKZbmCasYMW1PvbZkhKjTGhPMQricQS1WLcwfR82oHH1NHsVM3UaAfn1GiCDeh8fqYqByMqEm6x2SgCLJwaGFLKNmV6KoC5SxhcuvZUy8SS) and the [PurpleAir Classic SD Edition](https://cna.st/affiliate-link/5imWxNx3uP4SBwGErF2V6SP1usBPFhRJSZ5quv759mjokfZDhdYZtydCDj2yK8bMCg2H35coipFdeJvtxhZkduCrLcFfh96MYdqpourK4T7Le8mWZHgRcLTh9dsGw4RxqcPDjy8mdFytdHaoE7EKk5d2PYq9tgiZn2nuz4ckn2XxCKwsVc9R8oZ5MuTvrdN1LMS785DqEwgNiF6pXeuRmFymXphKE5NnF9ujwfJ4RGFiK6ZygnntA9P3UZNg63RUwZyFWD3N3o2DRxqkye3fSzzvEk4wEV4), which is able to store data without Wi-Fi. The one I have is PurpleAir's new Touch Indoor Air Quality Monitor. Its simple white oval plastic orb reminds me of a decorative, color-changing night light. The PurpleAir Touch measures PM 2.5. If an air quality monitor can measure only one thing, it should be PM 2.5. PurpleAir uses the same color scale for the US Air Quality Index, or [AQI Scale](https://www.airnow.gov/aqi/aqi-basics/). The Touch’s inner LED ring glows green for good air, yellow for moderate, orange for unhealthy for sensitive groups, red for unhealthy, purple for very unhealthy, and deep violet/maroon for hazardous. Depending on the number of taps of your finger, the glow gets brighter or turns off; it’s adjustable.

PurpleAir told me an app is in the works, but for the moment it's the PurpleAir website that has its “community scientist”-sourced map. I was confused the first time I looked at PurpleAir’s map, because it included both indoor and outdoor monitors. And while there the specific model of monitor pops up when you click the number, I’m not sure if I care that Zoe’s indoor air quality in Brooklyn is awesome or that Zander must have burned his muffins in Queens. I think including the indoor monitor data on the map muddies the user experience. To find out the actual AQI number that the PurpleAir Touch is reading, you need to register your Touch with PurpleAir and view it through PurpleAir's web portal. On its Real-Time PurpleAir Map I can see the circle that’s my Touch. There I can see the numbers for my living room. You can choose to be private or public. Looking at the map, I now want to get PurpleAir's outdoor monitor. And while configuring, registering, and checking a web portal is an extra step, I appreciate the simplicity of the Touch’s color system. The PurpleAir touch also makes a useful night light, though it was too bright for me at its highest setting.

## Best Budget Option for PM 2.5

![Govee Smart Air Quality Monitor an ovalshaped device with digital screen sitting on the top edge of a refrigerator](https://media.wired.com/photos/67ec4980ff12d808adb2aa48/4:3/w_320%2Cc_limit/Govee-Smart-Air-Quality-Monitor-Reviewer-Photo-SOURCE-Lisa-Wood-Shapiro.-(no-border).jpg)

### Govee Smart Air Quality Monitor

Amazon

Govee

At under $40, the Govee Smart Air Quality Monitor is one of the cheapest models I’ve tested. Govee has an easy-to-use and -pair app that can be further paired with Govee air purifiers, and an easy-to-read screen displaying the PM 2.5 along with temperature and humidity. I’ve been using the Govee Smart Air Quality Monitor for the past five months, and it likes to hang out at zero PM 2.5 much more than my other monitors. That said, I’ve moved it all around my home and its sensors spike when I’m cooking or using the toaster; it is just less sensitive than other far more expensive models. And even though it isn’t reading every piece of particulate matter flowing through the air, there’s a lot to be said for an affordable air quality sensor.

The Govee Smart is the size of a hotdog on a roll and comes in white or black. It has the option of displaying a digital clock (in military time) instead of the PM 2.5 reading, but I used it exclusively as an air quality monitor. The Govee Smart also has an air quality indicator color bar on the display; green for good, a confusing blue for moderate, then yellow, and red indicates bad air. If you’re an air quality monitor rookie, the Govee Smart might be the gateway air quality tool you’ve been seeking. I suggest placing your monitor outside your kitchen door or at least near your kitchen, always at breathing height—so at least 3 feet from the floor. Monitoring your PM 2.5 will give you a good idea if your cooking fumes are being pulled outside via exhaust or if they're wafting into your living space. And once you see your air quality, you can’t unsee it.

## Best Budget Option for PM 2.5 and VOCs

![Ikea Vindykstra Air Quality Sensor a white rectangular device with digital screen sitting on a white countertop](https://media.wired.com/photos/67ec4788629131183a895e9a/4:3/w_320%2Cc_limit/Ikea-Vindykstra-Air-Quality-Sensor-Reviewer-Photo-SOURCE-Lisa-Wood-Shapiro-(no-border).jpg)

Ikea

### Vindykstra Air Quality Sensor

Ikea

At under $50 and the size of a juice box, Ikea’s Vindstyrka packs a lot of features in a small package at a low price. The air quality monitor can be used on its own, paired with Ikea’s [Starkvind](https://cna.st/affiliate-link/D7sWyRTyBnUnTaHMR77KBhGEZ43eitLyDnnSdhpEcUKdgki15auhHzefFA6aVBmzDBpmzM24kbBqNuJufqZ9s672KHmgmeeava9pZNsNmKw3mPVmUnmqL2M9b9xU4HZ7b5Nz3kbtGM5xtcMeDHrTLG8wdsKSfHMk3VvRuqNSScpqTv5Q629LfivwrYCAXbsXBnLHjc) air purifier, and/or connected to Ikea’s Home smart app via the [Dirigera](https://cna.st/affiliate-link/gVLYTFHP77FHrVD2wiD1iV9aSrS1tCMVhfYiXSeG72uHKkhBGw7QELC5gBBhV2h1tnWguacd89BTDnKU9L82NyUTreTfoCm3B2ygXM9QYSt4oMKp5ESUjtS5DWB7d9gQ3xgRaBKrySrQ3saPGqo4BVrUTeQnfhYSkisG5ibYeqFjJ2B4gbW5JUw2tsDrTEsdfT5QU9pmJGR2fkezJS) hub. I was curious about the Vindstyrka back when I reviewed the Starkvind air purifier. And while I liked the modern look of the purifier, it was on the small side, and the only way I could connect it with my phone was to also use Ikea’s smart home hub. Still, like the Govee Smart Air Monitor above, an inexpensive air quality monitor is a great option for those who don’t want to spend hundreds of dollars.

Unlike the PurpleAir’s bold and colorful—though more expensive—monitors, the Vindstyrka has a demure, almost hard-to-read display. It’s easier to see when the button on the top turns on the light illuminating the display, but I have trouble reading the monitor if I’m across the room. It isn’t a font issue; instead, the difficulty lies with Vindstyrka’s matte gray early-version-of-Kindle screen. Ironically, the Vindstyrka is easiest to read in low light, as it auto-illuminates its display screen. Still, for the price, the Vindstyrka does more than monitor PM 2.5, temperature, and humidity. It also tracks VOCs or [volatile organic compounds](https://www.epa.gov/indoor-air-quality-iaq/volatile-organic-compounds-impact-indoor-air-quality#Health_Effects), those vapors and gases that can cause a host of health issues. And while the Vindstyrka display screen uses an arrow to indicate if VOCs are rising, falling, or remaining the same, it’s some indication. Though to take full advantage of the Vindstyrka, it’s best to use it with the Ikea Home smart app. And if you don’t mind being Ikea-bound, then the Vindstyrka is a solid little monitor at a low price.

## Best Composite Score

![Awair Element a white rectangular device with speaker in the front and digital display](https://media.wired.com/photos/67ec4a23cf02f10c5055e3df/4:3/w_320%2Cc_limit/Awair-Element-Reviewer-Photo-SOURCE-Lisa-Wood-Shapiro-(no-border).jpg)

### Awair Element

Amazon

Awair

When I first used the Awair five years ago, it didn’t bother me that it had its own composite scoring system. The Awair sensor uses a fan and laser sensor to detect PM 2.5; toxic chemicals known as volatile organic compounds, or VOCs; CO2; temperature; and humidity. Those individual scores go into Awair’s special algorithm that weights each of the five sensors and its actual toxic levels to humans over time, creating its color-coded scoring system. That means a score of 1 is a health hazard while a score of 100 means you’ve reached indoor air nirvana. The issue for me and my more air-aware self is when I see a reading of 100 on an indoor air monitor I think it means the air is bad. Yes, there is a tiny green light indicating overall good air, but still, it is confusing for me.

The score is the default setting on the display and can be easily overridden through the Awair app. By clicking on the gear in the upper right corner, you can change the display from the Awair Score to either temperature, humidity, CO2, chemicals, dust PM 2.5, or a clock. Though, even when I change the display, the indicator light will shine for the Awair score, so right now my PM 2.5 is 4—that’s good—but my indicator light is a confusing orange because of the high humidity that puts Awair’s composite score at a moderate 55. And as for the VOCs or toxic chemicals, it measures for a mixture as it cannot tease out a specific VOC like formaldehyde or benzene.

I liked the QP Lite Air Quality Monitor more than the QP Pro for the simple reason it had a clear display. Instead of a vague feeling that you’re getting tired or don’t feel great, one glance at the Lite’s screen and I knew to let the fresh air in. ## Why Do I Need an Indoor Air Quality Monitor?

![Hand holding square device with a screen showing air quality measurements](https://media.wired.com/photos/6695c7c7414b59b4fe4462a7/4:3/w_320%2Cc_limit/Air-quality-monitor-GettyImages-1213993541-Suebsiri-(cropped).jpg)

### Air quality monitors vs. air purifiers

By having a stand-alone air quality monitor, you will be able to see whether your air purifier is keeping up with the air in your physical space. In recent years, consumer indoor air quality monitors have improved in accuracy. After time those sensors can clog, giving off incorrect readings. I’ve used a hair dryer, set at the cool setting, to dislodge buildup in the sensors. Still, there seems to be a finite lifespan for the monitors I’ve used, as I’ve yet to have an air quality monitor work beyond four years. When placing or installing a monitor, look for the [breathing zone height](https://www.epa.gov/air-sensor-toolbox/guide-siting-and-installing-air-sensors#:~:text=Placement%3A%20As%20with%20outdoor%20sensors,measure%20of%20indoor%20air%20quality.), around 3 to 6 feet off the floor.

I am still sometimes surprised when I see a poor air quality reading on the monitor. I won’t smell or see anything, but my monitor can sense that there is too much bad air. My building is currently having construction, and on several occasions I’ve seen the [PM 2.5](https://www.epa.gov/pm-pollution/particulate-matter-pm-basics) reading go well beyond the safe level. At that point, I crank up the air purifiers. Since I started using both air quality monitors and air purifiers, my allergies—even in the spring—have been less severe. It’s made a difference in the quality of my sleep and my overall health. I no longer burn candles, cook without an exhaust fan, or spray air fresheners in my home, as I’ve seen how they negatively impact my indoor air in real time. Get one, and you might feel the same.

## Things to Know Before You Buy

![Purple Air Touch 2 an illuminated yellow device sitting on patterned surface](https://media.wired.com/photos/67ec4648adb7195af5a26e00/4:3/w_320%2Cc_limit/PurpleAirTouch2-Reviewer-Photo-SOURCE-Lisa-Wood-Shapiro-(no-border).jpg)

### What to look for

Even though [many current air purifier models](https://www.wired.com/gallery/best-air-purifiers/) have built-in air quality sensors, having a designated air quality monitor provides independent assurance. After I became air-quality enlightened, I can anticipate ways to mitigate bad air. Using air quality monitors taught me to turn on my kitchen exhaust fan and crank up my air purifier *before* I turn on my gas stove. In the past, I didn’t think much of a little smoke from cooking. Now I do everything I can to avoid it.

Before you purchase your own indoor air-quality monitor, here are some things to know: Sensors on three of the air quality monitors reviewed, the AirVisual Pro, Awair Element, and PurpleAir, all performed well in [an independent study](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0360132320300123?casa_token=aJXPc507uekAAAAA:3YKqJpv0i-AYtVkKA0uiJpGbc5VE2FgrbTWhEcgBXKWx8eLzO_osX1Uu329Wz3ueaULyK8zX-6E6). While low cost consumer air-quality monitors—let’s say, under $300—are far cheaper than those used by professionals, they are also less accurate and not easily calibrated. I have five air quality monitors in my home at the moment, and none are giving the exact same AQI, but they are close.

Consumer air-quality monitors [use sensors](https://www.epa.gov/indoor-air-quality-iaq/low-cost-air-pollution-monitors-and-indoor-air-quality#:~:text=Low%2Dcost%20air%20pollution%20monitors,such%20as%20temperature%20and%20humidity.) to detect tiny invisible particles in the air and/or other contaminants. [Some sensors use light scattering](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0360132320300123?casa_token=aJXPc507uekAAAAA:3YKqJpv0i-AYtVkKA0uiJpGbc5VE2FgrbTWhEcgBXKWx8eLzO_osX1Uu329Wz3ueaULyK8zX-6E6) to detect fine particulate matter known as PM 10, like the dust you can see in a sunbeam, and the invisible health hazard, PM 2.5. PM 2.5 is the size that we’re most concerned about, that infiltrates the deepest parts of the lungs and enters the bloodstream.
